content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 ,J Street• Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
{916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Kendra L. Camey 
General Counsel to OCSD 
555 Anton Blvd., Suite 1200 
Costa Mesa, CA 92626 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-073 
Dear Ms. Camey: 
May 17, 2016 
This letter responds to your request on behalf of the City of Fullerton and Greg Sebourn, a 
city councilmember and board member of the Orange County Sanitation District (the "Sanitation 
District") regarding conflict of interest restrictions that may apply to him. Please note that we only 
provide conflict of interest advice under the Political Reform Act {the "Act") 1 and Section 1090. 
We do not provide advice on other conflict of interest restrictions, if any, that could arise such as 
those governed by the common law. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re 
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), meaning that any advice we provide assumes the facts the 
requester provides to us are accurate. If this is not the case, then our advice could be different. 
Please note that after forwarding your request to the Attorney General's Office and the 
Orange County District Attorney's Office, we did not receive a written response from either entity. 
(See Section I 097. I ( c )( 4 ). ) Finally, we are required to advise you that the following advice is not 
admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor. (See Section 
1097.1 (c)(5).) 
QUESTIONS 
I. Does the conflict of interest provisions of the Act prohibit Mr. Sebourn from participating in 
the Sanitation Board's decision to award a contract to his employer, Stantec Consulting Ltd. 
("Stantec") or to a prime contractor whose bid will include Stantec as a subcontractor? 
2. Does Section I 090 prohibit the Sanitation District from entering into the contract with 
Stantec, or the prime contractor? Does the "remote interest" exception of Section 
109l(b)(l l) apply? 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 ofTitle 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSIONS 
File No. A-16-073 
Page No. 2 
1. Yes. It is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on 
Stantec which is a source of income to Mr. Sebourn. 
2. Yes, unless Mr. Sebourn is an engineer under relevant California law an<;! then the remote 
interest exception of Section 1091(b)(l 1) will apply as discussed below. 
FACTS 
Mr. Sebourn is employed by Stantec Consulting Ltd. ("Stantec") as a land surveyor. He is 
licensed by the state as a Professional Land Surveyor under the Professional Land Surveyors' Act 
("PLSA"). You state that the PLSA "suggests" that all licensed professional land surveyors may 
hold themselves out as "professional land surveyor, licensed land surveyor, land surveyor, land 
survey engineer, survey engineer, geodetic engineer, geomatics engineer, or geometronic engineer." 
Your office has confirmed with the California Department of Consumer Affairs Board for 
Professional Engineers, Surveyors, and Geologists that a licensed professional land surveyor is 
permitted to use any of those titles to describe his or her work. 
Stantec, which has divisions around the globe, provides a variety of professional consulting 
services, including planning, engineering, architecture, interior design, landscape architecture, 
surveying, environmental sciences, project management, and project economics services. 
Mr. Sebourn does not have a management or supervisory title and does not have any ownership or 
investment interest in Stantec. 
A division of Stantec for which Mr. Sebourn does not is considering bidding on a contract 
"either directly or as a subcontractor" in response to the District's Request for Proposal.2 
ANALYSIS 
Political Reform Act 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
A conflict of interest may arise only when it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a 
material financial effect on the official or his or her immediate family that is distinguishable from 
its effect on the public generally. (Section 87103.) 
Financial Interests 
Section 87103(c) provides that an official has an interest in any source of income, including 
promised income, that aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. Section 
87103( d) provides that an official has a financial interest in any business entity in which the official 
is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. Therefore, 
Mr. Sebourn has a financial interest in Stantec as a [business entity] and source of income. 
2 For purposes of the Act, we will assume that your use of the tenn bidding "indirectly" as a subcontractor means 
that when the prime contractor bids on the contract, Stantec would be identified in the bid as a subcontractor. 


Foreseeability and Materiality 
File No. A-16-073 
Page No. 3 
Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is 
"explicitly involved" in a decision. An interest is "explicitly involved" in a decision if the interest is 
a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's 
agency. (Regulation 1870l(a).) An interest "is the subject of the proceeding if the decision involves 
the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, 
or contract with, the financial interest . . .. " (Ibid.) Moreover, for any interest in a business entity 
(including a business entity that is a source of income to the official) explicitly involved in the 
decision, the financial effect of the decision is deemed material pursuant to Regulations 18702.l(a) 
and 18702.3(a)(4). Because Stantec is bidding on the contract, it is explicitly involved in a decision 
awarding the contract. Therefore, the financial effect of such a decision on Stantec is both 
foreseeable and material. 
Because the contract will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect 
Mr. Sebourn's source of income, he may not make, participate in making, or use his position to 
influence the Sanitation Board's decision to award the contract. Also, there are no facts to suggest 
that the legally required participation exception (Regulation 18705) or public generally exception 
(Regulation 18703) would apply. 
Section 1090 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. The prohibition applies regardless of 
whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. 3 Section 1090 is concerned 
with financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. 4 
We employ a six-step analysis to determine whether an official has a disqualifying conflict 
of interest under Section 1090. 
Step One - Three: ls there a public officer who is subject to Section 1090 participating in contract 
decisions? 
Section 1090 provides, in part, that "[ m ]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, 
judicial district, and city officers or employees shall no1 be financially interested in any contract 
made by them in their official capacity, or.by any body or board of which they are members." 
Mr. Sebourn, as a city councilmember and a Board Member of the Sanitation District, is a 
public officer subject to Section 1090. As a Sanitation District Board member, his participation is 
presumed in all contracts considered by the Sanitation District's Board while he is a member. 
3 1110mson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646. 
4 Stigall l'. Taft (1962) 58 Cal,2d 565,569. 


File No. A-16-073 
Page No. 4 
Section I 090 therefore prohibits any Board decisions while he is on the on the Board in which he 
has a financial interest. 
Step Four: Does Mr. Sebourn have a financial interest in the contract? 
Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest."5 Officials are deemed to have a financial interest in a contract if they might 
profit from it in any way.6 While Section 1090 does not define "financial interest," the courts have 
issued decisions applying this provision. "The certainty of financial gain is not necessary to create a 
conflict of interest . . .. The government's right to the absolute, undivided allegiance of a public 
officer is diminished as effectively where the officer acts with a hope of personal financial gain as 
where he acts with certainty.7 
Mr. Sebourn's employer from whom he receives income, Stantec, is seeking to obtain a 
contract with the District. While a contract with Stantec may not have an immediate or direct effect 
on Mr. Sebourn's income from Stantec, a contract decision would affect Stantec's finances and will 
contribute to his employer's financial health. Therefore, Mr. Sebourn's ongoing income from 
Stantec and the possibility of currying favor with Stantec impedes the government's rights to the 
absolute and undivided allegiance of a public officer. Absent an explicit statutory exception, 
Councilmember Sebourn will have a financial interest in the decision. 
Step Five: Does either a remote interest or a non-interest exception apply? 
As a general rule, when Section I 090 is applicable to one member of a governing body of a 
public entity, as here, the prohibition cannot be avoided by having the interested board member 
abstain; the entire governing body is precluded from entering into the contract. However, the 
Legislature has created various statutory exceptions to Section 1090's prohibition where the 
financial interest involved is deemed a "remote interest," as defined in Section 1091, or a 
"noninterest," as defined in Section 1091.5. 
You have specifically asked whether the District may contract with Stantec under the 
"remote interest" exception found in Section I 091 (b )( 11) which provides that the interest of an 
engineer, geologist, architect or planner employed by a consulting engineering, architectural, or 
planning firm is a remote interest, provided that he or she does not serve in a primary management 
capacity or as an officer or director of the firm. 
Stantec is a consulting engineering, architectural, or planning firm and Mr. Sebourn does not 
serve Stantec in a primary management capacity or as an officer or director ofStantec. Therefore, 
the exception will apply if Mr. ´eboum is an engineer, geologist, architect or planner. 
s People v. Honig,48 Cal.App.4111 289, 333 (1996). 
6 Ibid. 
7 Thomson, supra, at pp. 647-649; Stigall. supra, at p. 569; 86 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 138, 139 (2003); 70 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 45, 48 (1987). 


File No. A-16-073 
Page No. 5 
You have stated that Mr. Sebourn may fall within the exception as an "engineer" based on 
the licensing law applicable to Professional Land Surveyors8 and engineers. As stated above, we are 
not a finder of fact when rendering advice. Whether or not Mr. Sebourn is an engineer is a question 
of fact and law -- law outside the Act - and therefore, this detennination must be made by 
Mr. Sebourn. lf he detennines that he is an engineer, he would fall within the exception. 
You have also asked us .to consider whether the District may contract with a prime 
contractor which will use Stantec as a subcontractor. We do not address this question at this time 
because Mr. Sebourn's detennination of whether he is an engineer may render this question moot. 
Step Six: Does tlze mle of necessity apply? 
In limited circumstances, a "rule of necessity" has been applied to allow the making of a 
contract that Section 1090 would otherwise prohibit. (88 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 110 (2005).) 
Under the rule of necessity, a government agency may acquire an essential service, despite the 
existence of a conflict, when no source other than that which triggers the contract is available. 
(Eldridge v. Sierra View Hospital Dist. (1990) 224 Cal. App. 3d 311, 322.) Because you have 
provided no indication that the rule of necessity applies, we leave this determination to 
Mr. Sebourn. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
VJ:jgl 
8 Cal. Business and Professions Code Section 8751. 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
7t!AJ7 
By: 
Valentina Joyce 
Counsel, Legal Division
